perturbation.original_text: At the time of such notice, or if, a t any earlier time, we so direct (whether or not you determine to pursue a Transaction), you and your Representatives will, at your own expense, promptly return or destroy all copies of the Confidential Information upon our request (and, in any event, within five (5) business days after such request), except for that portion of the Confidential Information which consists of Derivative Information, which will be destroyed, and in the case of information stored in electronic form, it will be permanently erase d.
perturbation.changed_text: At the time of such notice, or if, a t any earlier time, we so direct (whether or not you determine to pursue a Transaction), you and your Representatives will, at your own expense, promptly return or destroy all copies of the Confidential Information upon our request (and, in any event, within one (1) business day after such request), except for that portion of the Confidential Information which consists of Derivative Information, which will be destroyed, and in the case of information stored in electronic form, it will be permanently erase d.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline to one business day may be unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with regulations requiring reasonable timeframes for data handling or legal review, especially considering the volume of confidential information potentially involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alberta Personal Information Protection Act, Section 36
perturbation.law_explanation: Alberta PIPA requires organizations to establish and comply with reasonable timeframes for responding to requests for access to or correction of personal information. The obligation to return or destroy all copies of confidential information within one business day might not afford the receiving party sufficient time to identify and segregate personal information, potentially leading to non-compliance with PIPA's requirements regarding access and correction requests.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for five business days to return or destroy confidential information, while the changed text reduces this timeframe to one business day. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this may be an unreasonably short timeframe, potentially conflicting with regulations requiring reasonable timeframes for data handling. The scraped law snippet discusses timeframes for responding to requests for access to or correction of personal information. While it's unclear what the context of the snippet is, and it is not directly related to the prompt, the altered contract's reduced timeframe potentially increases the risk of non-compliance, thus creating a legal contradiction as it directly impacts data handling.

perturbation.original_text: Without prior written consent of the Company, for a period of two (2) years from the date of this NDA (the “ Restriction Period ”), the Potential Bidder, its Repre sentatives, and its affiliates will not, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, employ, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of us or any of our affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this NDA by us in our operations, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of s uch solicitation, employment, or other contractual arrangements .
perturbation.changed_text: Without prior written consent of the Company, for a period of five (5) years from the date of this NDA (the “ Restriction Period ”), the Potential Bidder, its Repre sentatives, and its affiliates will not, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, employ, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of us or any of our affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this NDA by us in our operations, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of s uch solicitation, employment, or other contractual arrangements .
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under Alberta law, particularly if it unduly restricts an individual's ability to seek employment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Competition Act (Canada), R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34, section 45
perturbation.law_citation: Competition Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34, s. 45
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Competition Act primarily addresses agreements that unduly lessen competition, a five-year non-solicitation agreement could be scrutinized if it is viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade that significantly hinders competition in the labour market. Such a long period may be challenged as disproportionate to the legitimate business interests being protected, especially if it impedes the mobility of employees and limits their opportunities for advancement.

perturbation.justification: The original text specified a two-year non-solicitation period, while the changed text extends this to five years. The Perturbation Explanation states that this extension 'could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under Alberta law, particularly if it unduly restricts an individual's ability to seek employment.' The Law Explanation suggests that a five-year non-solicitation agreement could be scrutinized if seen as an unreasonable restraint on trade, hindering competition in the labour market. Although the scraped snippets don't explicitly define a maximum permissible duration for such agreements, the fact that the Competition Bureau investigates 'Restrictive trade practices' implies that overly broad restrictions are a concern. Given these points, increasing the non-solicitation period from two to five years creates a significant risk of being deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade, leading to non-compliance and thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any Notice delivered or transmitted as provided above shall be deemed to have been given and received on the day it is delivered or transmitted, provided that it is delivered or transmitted on a business day prior to 5:00 p.m. local time in the place of delivery or receipt. However, if the Notice is delivered or transmitted after 5:00 p.m. local time or if such day is not a business da y then the Notice shall be deemed to have been given and received on the next business day.
perturbation.changed_text: Any Notice delivered or transmitted as provided above shall be deemed to have been given and received on the day it is delivered or transmitted, provided that it is delivered or transmitted on a business day prior to 11:59 p.m. local time in the place of delivery or receipt. However, if the Notice is delivered or transmitted after 11:59 p.m. local time or if such day is not a business da y then the Notice shall be deemed to have been given and received two business days later.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the cut-off time for notice to 11:59 p.m. and extending the delay to two business days if delivered after this time could conflict with legal requirements for timely notification in certain contexts (e.g., breach of contract, legal proceedings), potentially prejudicing a party's rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Alberta Rules of Court specify requirements for service of documents in legal proceedings. While Rule 11.26 deals with electronic filing and service, the general principle is that documents must be served with sufficient time for the recipient to respond. Setting a very late cut-off time of 11:59 PM with a two-business day delay for notices delivered after that time, might lead to insufficient time to respond as per the rules, effectively delaying important deadlines in legal contexts covered by the Alberta Rules of Court.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a potential contradiction because extending the notification cut-off time to 11:59 p.m. and delaying the notice period to two business days could lead to insufficient time for the recipient to respond, potentially conflicting with legal requirements for timely notification as highlighted by the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. This delay might negatively impact legal proceedings or deadlines.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise provided herein, confidentiality and non -use obligations described in this NDA shall terminate upon the expiration of the Restriction Period.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise provided herein, confidentiality and non -use obligations described in this NDA shall terminate immediately.
perturbation.explanation: Terminating confidentiality obligations immediately could conflict with reasonable expectations and legal precedents regarding the protection of trade secrets and confidential information, potentially allowing for immediate misuse of sensitive data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alberta Trade Secrets Act, RSA 2000, c T-6
perturbation.law_citation: Alberta Trade Secrets Act, RSA 2000, c T-6
perturbation.law_explanation: The Alberta Trade Secrets Act provides legal protection for trade secrets. Immediately terminating confidentiality obligations under the NDA could allow a party to misappropriate and use trade secrets without any legal recourse, contradicting the protection afforded by the Act. The Act implies a reasonable duration of protection, which is violated by immediate termination.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'upon the expiration of the Restriction Period' to 'immediately' fundamentally alters the duration of confidentiality. The Law Explanation states that the Alberta Trade Secrets Act implies a reasonable duration of protection, which is violated by immediate termination. This suggests a contradiction because immediate termination could allow for misappropriation of trade secrets without legal recourse, undermining the protection that the Alberta Trade Secrets Act is intended to provide. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, stating that terminating confidentiality obligations immediately could conflict with reasonable expectations and legal precedents regarding trade secret protection.
